title: Gate/setup/mutation subprocess commands run with no timeout — a hung command deadlocks a worker slot and the whole farm run never terminates
evidence: The shared `run(cmd,args,cwd,opts)` helper (line 159) spawns with `{cwd, env, ...opts}` and resolves only on the child's `close`/`error` event — there is no timeout and no kill path. Unlike the outbound API call (which uses an AbortController + FARM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS, lines 556-582), every shell command goes through this un-timed helper: runGate (line 200), the per-task setup commands (via runGate, line 1063), the built-in mutation re-runs (`run(SHELL_BIN,[SHELL_FLAG,testCmd],wt,...)` line 936), and the pluggable FARM_MUTATION_CMD spawn (lines 891-902). If any such command hangs (a test that blocks on stdin, a watch/dev-server invocation that never exits, an interactive prompt), the awaiting `runTask` promise never settles. The main scheduler loop `while (pending.size>0 || running.size>0)` then awaits `Promise.race(running.values())` (line 1601), which never resolves, so the slot is occupied forever and the run hangs indefinitely with no report-finalize.
impact: A single buggy/flaky operator gate or setup command (not malicious — liveness, which the declared shell-trust exception does not cover) permanently consumes one of FARM_CONCURRENCY worker slots; if enough hang, the dispatcher wedges and never writes its final summary or exits, defeating the 'walk away' design.
recommendation: Add a per-command wall-clock timeout to `run()` (e.g. a FARM_GATE_TIMEOUT_MS, default a few minutes) that kills the child (child.kill, and on Windows tree-kill) and resolves with a non-zero RunResult tagged as a timeout, mirroring the AbortController discipline already used on the API path. Apply it to runGate, setup, and both mutation execution paths.
acceptance_criteria: A gate/setup/mutation command that never exits is killed after the configured timeout and surfaces as a gate failure / escalation rather than hanging the worker
The main scheduler always terminates and writes farm-report.* even when a task's gate command hangs
Timeout is configurable via env with a sane default and is <= or independent of FARM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_MS

title: defaultBuildImage ignores the `docker create` exit code, then docker-cp/dephash silently proceed on an empty checkout (swallowed failure -> wrong cache key)
evidence: In defaultBuildImage, the helper container is created with `spawnSync('docker',['create','--name',helper,...])` (lines 206-218) but the returned status is never inspected. If `create` fails (name collision with a leftover `ca-sbx-cp-*`, daemon hiccup, bad volume), execution falls straight into the try block: `spawnSync('docker',['cp',`${helper}:${APP_DIR}/.`,dir])` (line 220) — whose exit code is ALSO unchecked — leaves `dir` empty, `readManifests(dir,...)` returns `[]` (its own readdir is try/caught to `[]`, lines 251-255), `computeDepHash([])` yields a hash over nothing, and `buildOrReuseImage` proceeds with a degenerate cache key. The failure is fully swallowed: a sandbox is built whose dephash cache discriminator does not reflect the repo's real manifests.
impact: A partial docker failure during the checkout-materialization step does not surface as an error; instead the build silently uses an empty manifest set, producing a wrong/colliding dephash cache tag (AC-04/AC-05 cache correctness) and a sandbox image that may not match the repo's dependencies. The error is invisible to the caller.
recommendation: Check the `docker create` status; throw (or return a failed BuildResult) on non-zero with the captured stderr. Likewise check the `docker cp` exit code before computing the dephash, so an empty checkout is an explicit error rather than a silent empty-manifest build. The existing finally already removes the helper, so only the create/cp result checks are needed.
acceptance_criteria: A non-zero `docker create` exit aborts defaultBuildImage with a descriptive error including docker stderr
A failed `docker cp` (or an empty resulting checkout) is treated as an error rather than proceeding to computeDepHash over zero manifests
The helper container is still torn down on every path (unchanged)

title: H-09b/H-10b crypto/secret commit backstop fails OPEN when `git diff` errors or times out (asymmetric with H-14, which fails closed)
evidence: The H-09b/H-10b backstop computes `added = added_lines(cwd,'--cached')` (line 313). `added_lines` returns `''` on any git failure or 10s timeout (try/except -> '' at lines 147-156, and on returncode!=0 at lines 153-154). With `added=''`, `sensitive` is empty (line 316) and the entire crypto/secret-coverage block is skipped — the commit is allowed with no security-gate check. This is a fail-OPEN on the enforcement path. By contrast the sibling H-14 migration backstop in the same file fails CLOSED: `read_worktree` returning None (the analogous read failure) drives `uncovered.append(rel)` -> BLOCK (lines 369-372), and its marker-read failure also resolves to an empty approved set -> BLOCK. The two backstops handle the same class of read failure in opposite directions; only H-09b/H-10b silently passes.
impact: On a repo/host where `git diff --cached` errors or exceeds the 10s timeout (very large staged diff, slow/locked index, unusual filesystem), a commit introducing crypto or secret changes bypasses the H-09b/H-10b backstop entirely, with no diagnostic. The primary skill gate still exists, but this layer's whole purpose is to catch the no-skill-dispatched path.
recommendation: Make the git-read failure in this backstop fail closed (or at least fail loud): when `added_lines` cannot produce the staged diff for a `commit`, distinguish 'genuinely empty diff' from 'git read failed' (e.g. surface returncode/timeout) and BLOCK with a 're-run the security gate / git diff unavailable' message rather than silently allowing, matching the H-14 stance the file already documents as 'ambiguity resolves CLOSED'.
acceptance_criteria: A simulated `git diff --cached` failure/timeout on a commit no longer results in a silent allow of crypto/secret changes
Behavior is consistent with the H-14 backstop's fail-closed treatment of read failures
The documented fail-open exception remains scoped to hook STDIN parse only, not to the git-diff read inside the guard

title: farm main() finally runs `git worktree remove` on an undefined integrationWorktree when setup fails before assignment, masking the real error with a spawn TypeError
evidence: `integrationWorktree` is a module-level `let` (line 971) assigned only at line 1527, inside the try. If the integration-branch step throws first — `git(['branch','-f',...])` returns code!=0 and line 1525 throws `could not create integration branch ...` — `integrationWorktree` is still `undefined` when the `finally` (line 1620) runs `git(['worktree','remove','--force', integrationWorktree])`. That passes `undefined` as an argv element into `spawn` via the un-try-wrapped `run()` helper (line 161), and Node's spawn rejects non-string args with a synchronous TypeError thrown out of the Promise executor — which propagates out of the finally and replaces the original, actionable error. (writeReport on line 1619 runs first and is .catch-guarded, so the durable report is unaffected; observed:false — inferred from control flow, not executed.)
impact: A genuine setup failure (e.g. integration branch cannot be created) is reported to the user as an opaque spawn TypeError from the cleanup line instead of the real 'could not create integration branch' message, complicating diagnosis. Low blast radius (report already written).
recommendation: Guard the cleanup: only attempt the integration-worktree removal when `integrationWorktree` is set (`if (integrationWorktree) await git([...]).catch(()=>{})`), or initialize it before the try. Optionally make `run()` defensive against non-string argv.
acceptance_criteria: A failure before integrationWorktree is assigned surfaces the original error, not a spawn TypeError from the finally
Cleanup of the integration worktree is skipped (not crashed) when it was never created
